From Proto-Turkic *bodog (“paint, dye”), derived from Proto-Turkic *boda- (“to paint, dye”) (see боё (boyo)). Cognate with Old Uyghur [script needed] (boduɣ, “dye”), Karakhanid [script needed] (boδoɣ, “dye”), Bashkir буяу (buyaw), Kazakh бояу (boäu), Turkish boya.
From Proto-Turkic *tat. Compare to Southern Altai тат (tat), Kazakh тот (tot).

senses: diminutive of овца́ (ovcá): sheep, lamb (various senses)
etymology: From Proto-Turkic; cognate with Kazakh қой (qoi), Uzbek qoʻy, Azerbaijani qoyun, Turkmen goýun, Turkish koyun.
From өл- (öl-) + -үм (-üm). Cognates with Kazakh өлім (ölım), Turkish ölüm, Uzbek o‘lim.
From Proto-Turkic *kur (“belt”). Compare Southern Altai кур (kur).
From илүү (ilüü) + -гич (-gic). Cognate to Southern Altai илгиш (ilgiš), etc.

From Proto-Common Turkic *kadï-.
Cognate with
Old Turkic [script needed] (qaðïš, “belt”);
Kazakh қайыс (qaiys, “rawhide”);
Bashkir ҡайыш (qayış, “belt”),
Southern Altai кайыш (kayïš, “belt”)
Uzbek qayish (“belt”),
Khakas хаас (xaas, “belt”),
Yakut хатыс (qatïs, “leather strap/belt”),
etc.
From Proto-Turkic. Influenced by Old East Slavic.

senses: diminutive of топ (top) (female garment for the upper body)
dragée (a sweet or confection, originally as used to administer drugs, medicine, etc.)

etymology: A Mongolic borrowing: compare Mongolian хүрэн (xüren, “brown”) and see Persian کرند (korand) for more.

senses: doll, puppet (a toy in the form of a human)
etymology:
Cognate with
Old Uyghur [script needed] (quδurčuq, “doll”);
Bashkir ҡурсаҡ (qursaq),
Kazakh қуыршақ (quyrşaq),
Tatar курчак (qurçaq),
Southern Altai корчок (korčok, “humpback”),
Kumyk къурчакъ (qurçaq),
Uzbek qoʻgʻirchoq,
Uyghur қорчақ (qorchaq)/قورچاق (qorchaq),
Turkmen gurjak (“doll”), etc.

senses: dividend (а number that is to be divided by another)
dart (used in the game of darts)
etymology: Borrowed from Classical Persian نیزه (nayza, “spear, lance, javelin”).

senses: debit (an entry in the left hand column of an account or a sum of money taken out of a bank account)
dumping (selling goods at less than their normal price, especially in the export market)
doughnut, puff, bun (deep-fried piece of dough or batter)
dyer (one whose occupation is to dye)
duster, cleaner (person who dusts or cleans)

etymology: From Proto-Turkic *čï̄pïn, compare Azerbaijani çibin, Bashkir себен (seben), Crimean Tatar çibin.

Borrowed from Arabic خَبَر (ḵabar). Cognate with Karachay-Balkar хапар (xapar), Azerbaijani xəbər, Bashkir хәбәр (xəbər), Kazakh хабар (xabar), Khakas хабар (xabar), Kumyk хабар (xabar), Nogai хабар (xabar), Ottoman Turkish خبر (haber), Shor қабар, Tatar хәбәр (xäbär), Turkmen habar, Uyghur خەۋەر (xewer), Uzbek xabar, Chuvash хыпар (hyp̬ar), etc.
From Persian قیمت, from Arabic قِيمَة (qīma).

senses: Dalmatia (a historical region of Croatia, on the eastern coast of the Adriatic Sea)

etymology: Deverbal from бөлүү (bölüü, “to decompose, discriminate”). Compare to Southern Altai бӧлиш (böliš, “division”), Kumyk бёлмек (bölmek), etc.
From Proto-Turkic *kur (“belt”). Compare Southern Altai кур (kur).
From илүү (ilüü) + -гич (-gic). Cognate to Southern Altai илгиш (ilgiš), etc.

From Proto-Common Turkic *kadï-.
Cognate with
Old Turkic [script needed] (qaðïš, “belt”);
Kazakh қайыс (qaiys, “rawhide”);
Bashkir ҡайыш (qayış, “belt”),
Southern Altai кайыш (kayïš, “belt”)
Uzbek qayish (“belt”),
Khakas хаас (xaas, “belt”),
Yakut хатыс (qatïs, “leather strap/belt”),
etc.

senses: dystopia (an imagined state or society with dreadful quality of life, typically one that is totalitarian)

etymology:
From жалган (jalgan, “lie”) + -чы (-cı).
Cognate with Kazakh жалғаншы (jalğanşy).

senses: Detroit (a city in Michigan, United States)
